text: Large language models, or LLMs, are advanced computer programs that understand and generate human-like text. They work by analyzing vast amounts of text from the internet, books, and other sources during training. This training data helps them learn patterns in language, like grammar and context. When you give an LLM a prompt—such as a question or instruction—it uses this knowledge to predict and produce a relevant response. This process is called prompt engineering, where users craft inputs to get better outputs. LLMs have impressive capabilities, like writing essays or translating languages, but they have limits. They may make factual errors, lack real understanding, and reflect biases in their training data.
